speaker: interviewee
text: --exactly in winter. Middle of January. The winter was very strong. We felt chaos in the camp. Not only among prisoners, but most of among Wachtmeister, the staff. They said, we must leave. We must go so quickly because the Russian are coming here. And because I was working at this moment in magazine where we are dressing and this. Pack the dressing in some special wooden cases. Of course, later, we lost or left them, half of them, under the way. And we crossed Vistula. Vistula was frozen. Later I knew that some of my family recognized me, but I didn't. My mother told me that-- that they have seen me. I didn't. I remember only that two weeks we went by foot. Strong winter. And they want, always, if somebody try to escape, will be shot at. And don't try to sleep, because during this-- it was below 20 degrees there. When you would fall, then you will be frozen, will be finished. There were only some posts for sleeping in the farmers' sheds, let's say. I remember between the wooden it was so [? stretched ?] the wind came. But in the [INAUDIBLE], in the hallway, we covered so and we slept. And every day walked, walked, walked. When we walked then you see we have [? tear ?] from the frozen. You have seen over here. Everywhere. You look like the Christmas man. I never forget, because I have seen the other people. But they [? trusted ?] always, only a few days, only a few days. Then we will receive the rail cars, because probably they were here for soldiers maybe. And from maybe [INAUDIBLE] In-- in Polish language, [SPEAKING POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: They were waiting for us, the rail cars. We were there like the animals, without food, without drinking, without nothing. How long took this traveling? I don't know. Maybe two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I cannot remember exactly how I came to the [INAUDIBLE] prison, because I knew that this water, and on this island. I-- maybe it was night. We came by rail, then by ship night, and therefore, I remember only I was in the cell only also one man cell. Just a little, maybe second floor or third floor. And my job was there as a [GERMAN]. I have to-- I had never with pigs to do. But I think-- I thought maybe it will be very good job, because I have to feed the pigs. There will be something will be for me too. But it was not true. They gave only, you know the skins of potatoes, because near of our prison there was also mess for the Wachtmeister. And they ate completely different lunch and dinners like me. And only they gave me the skins of potatoes. I had to boil it for the pigs. I used sometimes I boiled it. And to boil it-- I never-- I took to my soup to make the soup so thick, thick, that I will sometimes I felt even stones, [POLISH]. I felt when I had this. And so when the-- it was a little-- I never forget even the from France, the prison, they picked this grass. A kind of grass. I don't-- Later it's a yellow flower on this. And they ate this. They told me, eat also. This is vitamins. It's very good to [INAUDIBLE]. After the war, I tried, but it's bitter [? food ?] for me. I remember the food that I ate in this. And the Wachtmeister said me, you must pick vitamins for the pigs. Of course the pigs were not for us. To their kitchen. They wanted to have good meat with many vitamins. I had not only grass, but also [INAUDIBLE]. It is terrible, you know. It was-- I don't-- every day, my hand was swollen. And one day, it was May, we heard that the war, it's finished. At 8 May, it was finished, but we were still at the prisons. It-- it was 15 or 14 May, 1945. I was picking this [INAUDIBLE] and the grass. I see three prisoners, one from [INAUDIBLE] who was together with me in Stuhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's coming, come on. We are escaping. Yes? But there's water. Don't worry, we know this. Come on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I left my cart, wooden cart with one rope, you know, for like this. And I joined them. We are going.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see there is around water. And one you call-- we called it [INAUDIBLE]. You know this [INAUDIBLE]? It was no [? this ?] on the [INAUDIBLE] and a rope. I was sitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two, they knew probably, they-- before, they made some test. Because it so quickly that when we find the other side of this ground, we jumped off of the boat. We jumped. Oh, we are free-- we are free. We are so lucky. Then we went a little, because we had still the prison dresses. On some places in the wooden. Somebody found it I think. I put it on the other way, my jacket, without the description of the prison, because there was written a prison number this and this. And I see a truck with soldiers. Among-- British soldiers. And among them were some Polish with the description Poland. Oh, I so lucky. I was so lucky. And I called, I am Polish. We are here. We are-- we are from Poland. First they gave to eat us, but it was a great mistake. After bad feeding, when they gave us immediately, first of all, everything is coming out. Then we suffer it. And they said, you see, you cannot-- the Polish soldiers say you cannot go to Poland. There are Russians. We have some places here for displaced persons, for the prisoners, and for the Jews in Bardowick. You will join them. You will wait when the situation will be clear, later, you will join with you family. And we went to the Bardowick. It was near of Luneburg. We were maybe there about two, three months. But the boys, the three of us together, oh, you see, we must do something. It is so boring here. Maybe we shall go-- because this village, a little village. I was dreaming about [INAUDIBLE]. I heard that it is divided. Germany is divided. One part belongs to British, to England, and one to America. And my dream was soon as possible go to American zone and maybe to Vienna. As I told you, my leg was always swollen. I had nothing. I bound it with some-- I had no bandage, no vitamins. And I walked so little. I couldn't walk. First I have to-- and before, I said, please, try we'll go [? to. ?] But there are no trains. No. And so from the Germans-- from British transport, I went to Hanover. And from Hanover, I founded-- I had luck, because I was born on the first [? January ?]. Everybody say, [POLISH]. I had luck, really. And I founded black man, American soldier. As you know, probably, in Holocaust, you will see there is the railway car. At the end, a small box, I don't know what for. Probably for some stopping or like this. And he was living there. He was. And [? I ?] said, I would like go to American zone. But I talked in German only. So I couldn't speak English. And he got-- he knew only some German language. OK. Then you stay here, he told me. And the three, they went to the car. And so from Munich-- from Hanover to Munich-- maybe three or four days. I don't know exactly. When we stopped, he gave me some eating from the tins, the rations, the soldier rations. He was so delicate. I told him me, prison. In this way, I talked to him. Oh, Good, boy. Yes. And then when we arrived in Munich, he said, you must go to [? Undra. ?] [? Undra ?] it is United Nation for this place. And I never forget this office was in Deutsches Museum. Deutsches Museum. I went immediately with our boys. We received our cards with the number. And I ask-- you see, but my leg is-- oh, they have seen. You must go first to the hospital. And they had no German. They sent me to the American hospital. And it was so-- no research, but the [INAUDIBLE], you know. It was so good. They gave me first some injection. I never forget that they [? placed ?] on the right. My leg could-- should be only hanged upstairs. You know. And they-- oh, no, and they cut it. They put it all-- because it was always open. They come always so-- in German, it's called [GERMAN] or gangrene, like this. It was-- if the war were longer, they cut it-- my leg should be maybe spoiled. And my leg would be cutted.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the American [? fixed ?] it. In three weeks, I was fit, healthy. I was so glad that I was alive and I could be-- but what have to do? They send me to a camp for displaced person. It was [GERMAN]. It is separate of Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living there without nothing to do. But I wanted to see the city, the Munich. As a young boy I wanted to see. And once I met in the Polish community, because immediately all Polish people they [? granted ?] some organization that worked against this communist government Poland. They said, stay better here. We wait. Maybe the situation will be clear. They printed also with English for self-learning. I received also because I wanted to go to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned from this book, by myself, English. And then in this community I met one gay. At the moment, I didn't know that. He said, you're living from? I live in Munich, in center.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can move to me. Yurick, his name. He is still alive. He is older than me. I have seen him even this year. We meet us in the park for exchange the bad experience, et cetera. And when I moved to him, I met many German soldiers. They wanted the girls. I'll never forget one day on the door was written, off limit. I never understood what this mean. But later I knew. It's forbidden to come here, probably because too many girls, German girls. They wanted to make love for some cigarettes, for some food. Because in the shop was nothing. They had plenty of mark, reichsmark. But for this reichsmark you couldn't buy nothing. And so on one day-- even on the door was written, off limits-- two tall American soldiers, one tall was Johnny. I not forget Johnny. He came to my-- what was my room, because even my house, [SPEAKING GERMAN] Berta-- she's not alive. I can say Berta [? Geibhart. ?] Even the address for [? I was, ?] I-- I know by mind. It was [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He came to me. He had seen that Yurick [INAUDIBLE] he makes his face so beautiful. I told, he has a rendezvous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, fine. With whom? With a black man. Oh, that's fine. Good. And then when he left, Yurick, he had some rendezvous. And he told me that he met some black man.

speaker: interviewee
text: The joint-- we had no real closing, only through-- how you call [GERMAN], you know, the [GERMAN]. He did this. And he wanted to do something. He was not homosexual, but wanted probably. He was very excited, because the women refused to do something. That was written, off limits. Maybe he want to like the black people. They-- there are many among them bisexual. They wanted this and this. And of course, he tried to do. But I couldn't. I didn't know this way. I hadn't still, at this moment, not the right experience. I wanted in this way. [? And I've ?] heard he would willing. He refused. He wanted the real, sophisticated way. I didn't do that. And then I said, you can take Yurick. No, I want you. I want you. I bring you tomorrow chocolate. I bring you tomorrow cigarette. But now he wanted. I made a little, but not in this way what he wanted. Of course, he came more and more. And then because I refused and he had also-- and when I was fit, I wanted to work. I could-- and then I went to the Polish, this communication. There was some kitchen help wanted. I went to the American kitchen together with him. We were together. In some picture I have, maybe here he is also there, Yurick in the picture. We worked there. Very good because instead of money-- the money was little value. We received PX rations exactly during the war like the American soldiers. I remember there was only one-- one kind of soap, life soap. Surely the young generation of Americans they do not know this. It was not smelling nice, but against the [INAUDIBLE] very good for skin. And then chocolate, very good. Apples. Fresh apples from California. In-- for German, it was money. Then I could deal. I could sell. I didn't smoke, so I sell the cigarette for some dress and shoes. And then, when I had the experience with-- with some knowledge with English, because I bought, I received food. And I grew up. I went to Dachau, to the officer's mess. I was the assistant waiter. My PX rations were still higher. I had many contacts, because among the American soldiers, even Polish immigrants were there. Once I met [? from ?] [? Torun. ?] He asked me also, don't go there. There are Russians. Be careful. Don't go there. But I say, then I go to Vienna. I will to-- I didn't tell him what the reason is. But he said, Russia, in Vienna, in Austria there are also Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and when I was working there, Yurick had experience because he worked in Munich as a forced-- as a displaced person. He worked in Munich in a manufactury for the war. So that he had many places.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had-- he knew some gays, German homosexuals. He told me the bad experience during the war, everything. Even our house [? Frau, ?] she knew. But she was tolerant. She knew everything. She said, the stupid Hitler, the best people are you. You are so intelligent. I knew before the war. She wanted to marry. She was single. And probably she wanted to marry somebody from the gay people. And before she appreciated so much. And then, I never forget. It was maybe 1947, May. I crossed the river of Isar, in Munich is Isar. And when I walked there, somebody crossed with a Jeep and looked me-- and asked me somebody in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't. I'm sorry. I don't speak. And then he asked me in German. Could you show me the way to the Ludendorff-Villa?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the first time I heard Ludendorff. And then I said, no. Then maybe-- then come on. But you know maybe the [? Maximillian-Brica. ?] Yes, I know the-- Then take us it. You show me the [? Maximillian. ?] When I-- when I went, and when I came and was in the car, he asked me maybe. I told him I am not German. I am not German. No, I am Polish. I am displaced person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, then we shall go to the Ludendorff-Villa. Why? But you asked for the Ludendorff. Now you are taking me. I knew. I wanted only to meet you. Oh, it's pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me beautiful villa. He was also Jew. He was Jew. He was bisexual. He said to me everything. I met him maybe still once day more, maybe three times. And he went, you are so nice fellow. My wife will be-- we have no children. We are ready to adopt you. You'll be very glad. In America, you will finish high school. I educate you. I make you as a mensch. But you must receive from your mother permission. You must receive the [INAUDIBLE] bonded documents, because I want to adopt you. And we were also in the consulate. And the consulate said, yes, everything is OK. Only we need this from parents. Oh my god. I find it in myself to go. America was my dream country. I want to go. There were some times when I was in prison, very bad time, depression. I was dreaming. I will change, I said. Fly. I fly to America over all countries. But then my mother was waiting for me, suffering. And at this moment, I received already letter. Because she wrote-- no, first I gave in the Polish in the radio, you know, that I am alive. If my family is alive. And she has to write. This my address private is this. Munich is [GERMAN]. And I received a letter from her. She was so glad. My boy, you are alive. I want you, so you must come home. And then I was thinking, now I cannot do this. It should be very bad for me. I chose the beautiful country. She suffered. And-- and then, at the-- in June 1947, I left Germany for Poland. Maybe later, I-- I-- I regret it. Yes. But I never regretted that I joined my mother. That I did so much as I could for her. [SOBBING] And this was my life. But maybe you will want to know how I came to write the story about me. When I was ill, but not only [? dilect. ?] [? Dilect ?] with the American is excellent. Never more. But I suffered labor and harm. And we had in Poland very bad time. It was martial law. I couldn't work. I went as an early pensioner, free [? rent. ?] And I tried to write the letters for the indemnification from Germany government. I wrote to the consulate. First letter, I received very nice, in very kind way, a reply that the-- the letter has been sent to the financial minister, and I have to wait for the reply. I thought it will be everything, OK. But from the financial minister, I received in three sides of letter. We give once to the communist government so many million of marks. And then, go to them. Ask by them. Apply you indemnification. I couldn't do that. First of all, as a homosexual, I couldn't tell them that I was in prison as a homosexual. I couldn't find a job. Maybe later, if I have more time, then I will tell you also. And so I wrote once more. Please give me even symbolic one mark. I will see that you regret it for my [GERMAN], for my young years. Because I spent-- this is 5 years taken from my life for nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- for-- for what? I didn't kill. I didn't steal. I didn't did nothing wrong. Only the Germans did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- and give me only one mark as satisfaction. I forgive you. I forgive all Germany for this. You know, he wrote me so in bad form letters. I have all with me. That don't try to-- we wrote you. We have no any rights to give you any indemnification. If you write anymore, we destroy this letter. It will be go to the basket. Then I think, no. First, I wanted to write to the United Nations. But I think maybe it's too high. They will not understand me. Then I found an address in Germany. In the parliament there was organization. The-- it was the [GERMAN]. It was the member of this parliament for homosexual prisoners. And I wrote to him. And in this very difficult situation, I didn't receive the indemnification. I suffered so. I wrote him my-- in short way. I received very kind reply. He regretted also very much that I was so-- treated by this-- by the German government. And he asked me whether I agree with some collection for me. They wanted to collect money from all. And I said, no. Don't do that. It is like [GERMAN] in German language. I am not beggar. I have my proud. Don't do that. I will find my-- in other maybe way. And I was waiting. And one day, I received a letter. Rather a thick letter. It was in my post box where I'm living. I'm opening the letter and there was a book. The book I have. 'Home for Young Jewish People in Israel.' The name of the writer was Lutz van Dijk. And I'm wondering what for I received this book. But then I read in the letter. Dear this and this. I'm enclosing my book. I am a writer. I- I would-- I read-- I heard your story, life story. It is so interesting story. I would like to write a book on you. I enclose 20 marks for you as a stamps. Please write me whether you agree for this book. I read. I have seen. First, I read this book. It was the style. Very correct. Very nice for young readers. I think maybe it will be good. So I wrote to him. I agree. I-- I bought used typewriter. And I typed him my story. He-- he wrote me also some question. We collaborated on one book as my story. I wanted to show all the world how I suffered for nothing. For nothing. Without any reason. And the German government, they treated me in this way. Therefore, I wrote to-- and I wrote this book. And when the project of this book was finished, the writer of this book came to Warsaw. With this project I read once more. Of course, it was difficult to read, but I read. I gave my signature. I agree with the publisher of the-- publish this book. Oh, he brought me-- because it was in December, he brought me some gifts for Christmas from his home, from some friends. Because the first book was written for that. He brought from his friend, and he gave me also the birthday present, gifts. And so later, I received letters from, not only from readers, but also from homosexual. And he said, yes, they knew that you are in demand. You must be operated. I had-- I suffered also skin cancer skin. And in this martial time, no medicine. I had to do only private, and it costed a lot of money. They paid for me. They collected this. Only-- and it was also very touched for me. Like for them, my story was touched. Because they-- they are wondering that me, as a homosexual in Polish, I-- I'm forget. Forgive them, and I give the hand. As a Catholic, I forgave. It is in our prayer. You must forgive to everybody. Only with forgetting, especially their people who beaten me, I cannot forget it. And so it came to this book.
